---
title: "Coding & Collaboration at emLab"
subtitle: "The updated SOP: formatters, branch-based development, and code review"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    navigation-mode: linear
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: false
    self-contained: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .new-badge {
          background: #2aa198;
          color: #fff;
          font-size: 0.42em;
          padding: 0.12em 0.55em;
          border-radius: 0.4em;
          vertical-align: middle;
          margin-left: 0.45em;
          font-weight: 700;
          letter-spacing: 0.06em;
        }
        .reveal a { color: #2aa198; }
        .reveal a:hover { color: #1f7f78; }
        .semi-transparent { opacity: 0.4; }
        .no-bullets, .no-bullets li {
          list-style: none !important;
          margin-left: 0 !important;
          padding-left: 0 !important;
        }
        .no-bullets li::before, .no-bullets li::marker { content: none !important; }
        .reveal .small { font-size: 0.75em; }
        .reveal table { font-size: 0.7em; }
        </style>
---

## Why this matters

::: {.notes}
Frame the whole talk: code at emLab informs real decisions, so errors carry real costs. Two goals drive everything else: accuracy and reproducibility.
:::

Our analyses go to policymaker, implementors, funders, and the scientific community. A mistake in the code is a mistake in the science.

Two goals drive all the code we write:

::: {.incremental}
- **Accuracy** — the code does what we think it does.
- **Reproducibility** — someone else (or future you) can re-run it and get the same result.
:::

## 'Universal requirements' vs. 'best practices'

::: {.notes}
Clarify the two tiers. Universal requirements are the shared baseline everyone follows. Best practices are what we aspire to, adapted per project.
:::

The SOP distinguishes two tiers:

::: {.incremental}
- **Universal requirements** — the baseline everyone follows, on every project. This talk will focus 
- **Best practices** — what we aim for, adjusted per project.
:::

::: {.fragment}
This talk is mostly about the requirements, especially the new ones.
:::

## All universal requirements

::: {.notes}
GitHub is the platform for all coding work. The first two have been in place; the last three are new this revision.
:::

All code at emLab must:

::: {.incremental}
- Live in a repo under [emlab-ucsb](https://github.com/emlab-ucsb) — with a README and logical structure.
- Have every change tracked in Git with clear commit messages.
- [[NEW]]{.new-badge} Use a [code formatter](../03-code/code-styling.qmd) — Air (R) or Ruff (Python), run on save.
- [[NEW]]{.new-badge} Follow [branch-based development](../04-gh/05-branches.qmd) — no direct commits to `main`.
- [[NEW]]{.new-badge} Go through [code review](../03-code/code-review.qmd) via pull requests, planned at project start.
:::

# 1 · Code formatters {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Style is documentation

::: {.notes}
Set up the philosophy before the tooling. 
:::

Code at emLab needs to be durable — we share code publicaly, hand off projects between team members, take leave, and return to our own work years later.

Style isn't aesthetic — it's how your work stays usable:

::: {.incremental}
- diffs reflect *real* changes, not whitespace noise
- a collaborator can read unfamiliar code without reverse-engineering it
- future you can pick up where you left off
:::

## Formatters are required [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
This is the key new requirement in the Code chapter. A formatter handles layout automatically — no manual spacing, no comma-by-comma fixes. Set it up once.
:::

A formatter handles all the spacing, indentation, and line-break decisions automatically. Set it up once and forget about it.

::: {.callout-important}
Formatters are **required** for all new code development.
:::

Both tools run on save, so there's nothing to remember.

## Air — required for R [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
Air is built by Posit and enforces Tidyverse style. Two steps: install the extension, enable format-on-save.
:::

**Air** is the required R formatter (built by Posit, enforces Tidyverse style).

1. In Positron / VS Code, open Extensions (`Cmd+Shift+X`) and install **"Air"** (by Posit).
2. Turn on format-on-save in your `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "[r]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "posit.air",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  }
}
```

## Ruff — required for Python [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
Ruff is the formatter and linter for Python — extremely fast, replaces several older tools. Same pattern: install, enable on save.
:::

**Ruff** is the required Python formatter and linter — it replaces several older tools.

1. Install the **"Ruff"** extension from the marketplace.
2. Enable format-on-save:

```json
{
  "[python]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "charliermarsh.ruff",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.codeActionsOnSave": {
      "source.fixAll.ruff": "explicit",
      "source.organizeImports.ruff": "explicit"
    }
  }
}
```

## On RStudio

::: {.notes}
Air isn't tightly integrated in RStudio, but styling is still required. Mention the styler fallback and the "end files with a newline" setting.
:::

Styling is still required in RStudio, where Air is less integrated. Use the `styler` package:

::: {.incremental}
- `install.packages("styler")`, then **Addins → "Style active file"** before each commit.
- **Tools → Global Options → Code → Saving** → check *"Ensure that source files end with a newline."*
:::

::: {.aside}
Air can also be wired into RStudio as an external CLI formatter — see [Posit's instructions](https://posit-dev.github.io/air/editor-rstudio.html). Full detail: [Code styling](../03-code/code-styling.qmd).
:::

# 2 · Branch-based development {background-color="#2aa198"}

## The rule [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
State the requirement plainly. All development happens on a branch; we don't commit to main.
:::

> **All code development happens on a separate branch. We don't commit directly to `main`.**

::: {.incremental}
- Your work stays isolated until it's ready.
- A dead end never touches `main` — abandon the branch and move on.
- Branches are what make code review possible.
:::

## What a branch is {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Use the SOP's own diagram. A branch is a named pointer to a snapshot that then moves forward independently.
:::

Git history is a sequence of snapshots:

```
A --- B --- C --- D   (main)
```

A **branch** is a named pointer that moves forward independently:

```
A --- B --- C --- D           (main)
                  \
                   E --- F    (my-branch)
```

`main` is untouched until you explicitly merge.

## Why not commit to `main`?

::: {.notes}
Main is the stable, reviewed record — always runnable. Branch-based dev creates a checkpoint for review. Applies even solo. At emLab, a ruleset enforces it.
:::

`main` should always run. It's the reviewed, stable version of the project.

::: {.incremental}
- Branching creates a checkpoint: work → PR → review → merge.
- This matters even when you're working alone — reviewing your own diff before merging catches things.
- Most repos block direct pushes to `main`; if you hit that error, you need a branch.
:::

## The workflow {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Walk the six steps end to end. All of this also works through the GUI in Positron, VS Code, and RStudio.
:::

1. **Start from an up-to-date `main`** — `git switch main && git pull`
2. **Create a branch** — `git switch -c add-spatial-aggregation`
3. **Work and commit** in logical units
4. **Push** — `git push -u origin <branch>`
5. **Open a pull request** and merge
6. **Delete the branch** after merge

::: {.aside}
Every step also works through the Git panel / branch picker in Positron, VS Code, and RStudio — no terminal required.
:::

## Keep branches small and current

::: {.notes}
Two practical habits: pull from main periodically to keep conflicts small; keep branches focused and short-lived.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- **Stay current:** pull from `main` periodically — the longer a branch diverges, the worse the merge.
- **Stay focused:** one branch, one task. Short-lived branches are easier to review and less likely to conflict.
- Big task? Break it into branches that build on each other.
:::

::: {.aside}
Full detail: [Working with branches](../04-gh/05-branches.qmd).
:::

## Issues

::: {.notes}
Issues are the right place to flag bugs, questions, and planned work — before writing code. They keep conversation out of Slack and create a searchable record tied to the repo.
:::

Use an Issue to flag a bug, propose a change, or raise a question — before writing any code.

::: {.incremental}
- Keeps the conversation in the repo, not in Slack.
- Issues can be referenced from commits and PRs ("Closes #42").
- When you come back to the code later, you'll know why decisions were made.
:::

::: {.aside}
Full detail: [Working with Issues](../04-gh/04-issues.qmd)
:::

# 3 · Pull requests & code review {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Pull requests [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
A PR is a request to merge one branch into another, and the standard venue for review. All changes to main come through a PR.
:::

A **pull request** is how you propose merging your branch into `main` — and how code review happens.

::: {.incremental}
- GitHub shows exactly which lines changed.
- Collaborators can comment, ask questions, and approve.
- Leaves a record of what changed and why — useful when something breaks six months later.
:::

**All changes to `main` come through a PR.**

## Opening a PR

::: {.notes}
The four fields. Emphasize a good title and description, linking issues, and draft PRs.
:::

Push your branch, then open a PR. Fill out:

::: {.incremental}
- **Base:** the branch you're merging *into* — usually `main`.
- **Compare:** your feature or analysis branch.
- **Title:** a clear, specific summary of what changed.
- **Description:** what changed, why, and what a reviewer should focus on. Link issues ("Closes #42").
:::

::: {.fragment}
Not ready for review? Open a **Draft PR**.
:::

## Reviewing a PR {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Three outcomes. Distinguish blocking concerns from suggestions.
:::

A reviewer looks at both the **code** and the **analytical approach**. GitHub gives three options:

::: {.incremental}
- **Comment** — feedback without approving or blocking.
- **Approve** — there may still be some minor issues to resolve, but the author should be able to handle these without another re-review (*Generally recommended!*)
- **Request changes** — large issues must still be resolved, and a **mandatory re-review** is necessary before merge.
:::

::: {.fragment}
In practice, we recommend using **Comment + Approve** on small, low-risk PRs — reserve "Request changes" for substantive concerns.

Flag **blocking concerns** separately from **suggestions**.
:::

## Plan review up front [[NEW]]{.new-badge}

::: {.notes}
The biggest cultural shift. Code review is planned at project start, not reactive. Answer four questions. Budget 10-20% of coding time.
:::

The biggest shift: review is **planned at the start of a project**, not triggered by a journal requirement or an error someone found.

A plan answers four questions:

::: {.incremental}
- **What** gets reviewed · **when** · **by whom** · **to what standard**
:::

::: {.fragment}
Budget **10–20% of coding time** for it. Bring it up at [kickoff](../03-code/code-review.qmd).
:::

## Types of of code review

::: {.notes}
Two dimensions: who reviews (self vs. peer), and what they're checking. Accuracy and efficiency apply to every PR. Reproducibility is a separate final stage for publications.
:::

**Pull requests** 

| | Timing | What to check | Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-review** | Ongoing; Before every PR; revisit after receiving PR comments | Read your own diff as if you're the reviewer | Always |
| **Team member peer review** | Ongoing; for every PR | Accuracy, logic, efficiency | Whenever there's more than one researcher on the project |

::: {.fragment}
**Reproducibility check** 

| | Timing | What to check | Required? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External peer reviewer**  | End of project and before submission; again before publication | A designated reviewer re-runs the full analysis independently and regenerates all outputs| Publications and external-facing deliverables |
:::

## Best practices {auto-animate="true"}

::: {.notes}
Split responsibilities. Authors make review easy; reviewers are specific and prioritize correctness.
:::

**If your code is being reviewed:**

::: {.incremental .small}
- Keep PRs small and focused. Write a clear description.
- Make sure it runs end-to-end before requesting review.
- Respond constructively — keep the discussion in the PR.
:::

## Best practices {auto-animate="true"}

**If you're reviewing:**

::: {.incremental .small}
- Know what kind of review is being asked for.
- Be specific; label things "Required:" vs. "Nit:".
- Prioritize correctness over style. For reproducibility review, actually run the code.
- Be timely — a stale PR gets harder to merge as the rest of the project moves on.
:::

## Code review is a collaboration

::: {.notes}
Close the section on norms. Review is non-adversarial, a contribution worth recognizing, and needs protected time.
:::

::: {.incremental}
- Review is **not a performance evaluation** — it's how we catch errors before they matter.
- Time spent reviewing is time spent on the project. It should be treated that way.
- PIs and PMs: if review isn't in the timeline and budget, it won't happen.
:::

::: {.aside}
Full detail and the plan template: [Code review](../03-code/code-review.qmd).
:::

# The rest of the SOP {background-color="#2aa198"}

## Also worth knowing

::: {.notes}
Lighter touch on the other new code material — mention and point to the chapters.
:::

More new material in the Code section :

::: {.incremental}
- **Basic Git** — [setup](../04-gh/01-setup.qmd), [commits](../04-gh/03-commits.qmd), and [Issues](../04-gh/04-issues.qmd).
- **[Documentation](../03-code/code-documentation.qmd)** — comment the *why*; Roxygen2 for functions.
- **[Functional programming](../03-code/functional-programming.qmd)** — don't repeat yourself; `map`/`apply` over loops.
- **[Reproducibility](../03-code/index.qmd#reproducibility)** — [`renv`](../03-code/package-reproducibility.qmd) for packages, [`targets`](../03-code/pipeline-reproducibility.qmd) for pipelines.
- **Quarto** — [chunks](../05-quarto/chunk-best-practices.qmd), [figures & references](../05-quarto/figures-tables-references.qmd), and [presentations](../05-quarto/presentations.qmd) (like this one!)
:::
:::

## Recap

::: {.notes}
Tie it back to the three new requirements.
:::

Three new universal requirements:

::: {.incremental .no-bullets}
- ✓ **Format on save** — Air for R, Ruff for Python.
- ✓ **Branch-based development** — never commit to `main`.
- ✓ **Code review through pull requests** — planned from the start.
:::

::: {.fragment}
All three exist for the same reason: accurate, reproducible science.
:::

## Find everything in the SOP

The full Standard Operating Procedures live at:

[**emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP**](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/SOP/)

::: {.aside}
Source: [github.com/emlab-ucsb/SOP](https://github.com/emlab-ucsb/SOP)
:::

## Questions?
